name: Custom Clients
summary: Standard for abstracting data fetchers (HTTP, GQL, WS).
description:
Design Philosophy

A CustomResourceClient decouples the application from the transport layer. It aligns with the Apollo Link pattern but generalized for any request type.

Interface Protocol

interface CustomResourceClient {
    // Returns a cold observable (Consumable)
    execute(
        operation: ResourceOperation
    ): Consumable<ResourceResult>;
}

interface ResourceOperation {
    kind: 'query' | 'mutation' | 'subscription';
    key: unknown[]; // Normalized ID
    context: Record<string, unknown>;
}

name: Custom Middleware
description:
Design Philosophy

Middleware allows cross-cutting concerns to be composed independently of the client. Aligns with Axios Interceptors but stackable via dependency injection.

Interface Protocol

type MiddlewareNext = (
    req: ResourceOperation
) => Consumable<ResourceResult>;

interface CustomMiddleware {
    process(
        req: ResourceOperation,
        next: MiddlewareNext
    ): Consumable<ResourceResult>;
}

name: Custom Cache
summary: Standard for defining validity and retention policies.
description:
Design Philosophy

Defining how long data is fresh should be separate from where it is stored. This protocol standardizes concepts from HTTP Cache-Control and TanStack Query.

Interface Protocol

interface CustomCachePolicy {
    mode:
        | 'cache-first'
        | 'network-only'
        | 'stale-while-revalidate';
    maxAge: number; // ms
    staleWhileRevalidate: number; // ms
    key: (op: ResourceOperation) => string;
}

name: Custom Tracer
summary: Protocol for observability and request tracing, decoupled from the implementation.
description:
Design Philosophy

Tracing (Lifecycle) is distinct from Tracking (User Behavior).

  • Tracing: Observes the provenance of the data (e.g., "Request used Schema V2, took 50ms, transformed by Pipe X").
  • Tracking: Observes the outcome for business logic (e.g., "User purchased item").

This protocol focuses on Context Propagation (via W3C Trace Context) and collecting safe execution metadata (Schema, Transformations) to establish provenance, independent of the storage backend.

Interface Protocol

interface CustomTracer {
    // Wraps execution to measure duration
    trace(
        op: ResourceOperation,
        execute: () => Consumable<ResourceResult>
    ): Consumable<ResourceResult>;

    // Injects tracing headers (W3C Trace Context)
    injectContext(op: ResourceOperation): void;
}

name: Custom Pagination
summary: Standard for iterating over resource collections (Cursor, Offset, Page).
description:
Design Philosophy

Every API handles lists differently: page/limit, offset/limit, or cursor/after. This standard normalizes the iteration process into a simple next() protocol, so a UI consumer — the Collection Operations intent's page dimension — can drive any list without knowing the underlying strategy.

Strategy & UX Consequences

The strategy is a technical data-fetch choice, but it is not UX-neutral: it dictates which pagination affordances the page dimension can offer. The deciding factor is whether a total is available.

  • offset / page — recompute a window against the full set, so a total is available: this enables jump-to-page (numbered, pageMode: paged) and a rangeLabel ('showing 21–40 of 500'). Trade-off: under concurrent inserts/deletes the window shifts, so rows can be skipped or duplicated across pages.
  • cursor — fetches the next N rows after a stable key, so it is stable under writes, but it exposes no total and only next()/previous(). It therefore cannot drive jump-to-page or rangeLabel, and forces the intent to pageMode: append or prev/next.

Cross-layer constraint: jump-to-page and rangeLabel require a known total, so they pair only with an offset/page strategy — a cursor strategy forces append/prev-next. This is the same fact the Collection Operations intent states from the UX side, kept consistent here on the protocol side.

Interface Protocol

interface CustomPagination<T> {
    // The current slice of data
    readonly items: T[];

    // Metadata
    readonly hasNext: boolean;
    readonly hasPrevious: boolean;
    readonly total?: number;       // present for offset/page; absent for cursor

    // Navigation
    next(): Promise<CustomPagination<T>>;
    previous(): Promise<CustomPagination<T>>;
}

type CustomPaginationDefinition =
    // total available -> drives jump-to-page + rangeLabel; window shifts under writes
    | { strategy: 'offset'; limit?: number }
    | { strategy: 'page'; limit?: number }
    // no total, next()/previous() only -> forces append / prev-next; stable under writes
    | { strategy: 'cursor'; limit?: number; key?: string };

name: Custom Resources
summary: Standard for defining resource entities and their data strategies (Registry).
description:
Design Philosophy

Just like customElements maps a tag name to a class, customResources maps a business entity (e.g. 'user', 'product') to its resource configuration. This allows the application to simply request users without knowing if it comes from GraphQL or REST.

Interface Protocol

interface CustomResourceRegistry
    extends CustomRegistry<ResourceDefinition> {

    readonly localName = 'customResources';

    define(name: string, def: ResourceDefinition): void;

    // Returns the config wrapper for a resource
    get(name: string): ResourceHandle;
}

interface ResourceDefinition {
    // The Resolver
    identify?: (params: unknown) => unknown[];
    // e.g. OpenTelemetry
    tracer?: CustomTracer;
    // Strategy (Offset/Cursor)
    pagination?: CustomPaginationDefinition;
}
